description: A timeline or overview diagram showing the transfer learning paradigm in NLP, illustrating how pre-trained models (like BERT, ELMo, ULMFiT) represent an inflection point in NLP, analogous to ImageNet's impact on computer vision.
teaching_value: Helps students understand the historical context and significance of transfer learning in NLP, drawing a parallel to the well-known ImageNet moment in computer vision to anchor the concept.
when_to_show: Show at the very start of a lesson on BERT or NLP transfer learning to motivate why these models matter and situate them historically.
suggested_caption: Transfer learning transforms NLP, mirroring ImageNet's impact on vision. (Alammar)

description: A diagram showing BERT used for spam classification: input text flows into the BERT model, and a classifier head on top outputs a spam/not-spam prediction.
teaching_value: Gives students a concrete, relatable use case (spam detection) to understand how BERT is applied to sentence classification via fine-tuning, making the abstract architecture tangible.
when_to_show: Show when introducing how BERT is applied to downstream tasks, before diving into architecture details, to ground students in practical usage.
suggested_caption: BERT fine-tuned for spam classification with a classifier head. (Alammar)

description: A side-by-side comparison of BERT Base and BERT Large model sizes, showing differences in number of encoder layers, hidden units, and attention heads.
teaching_value: Allows students to directly compare the two BERT variants and understand how scaling up layers, hidden size, and attention heads affects model capacity.
when_to_show: Show when introducing BERT's architecture section to help students distinguish the two model sizes and understand the role of hyperparameters.
suggested_caption: BERT Base vs. BERT Large: encoder layers, hidden units, attention heads. (Alammar)

description: A diagram showing the stacked encoder layers inside BERT Base (12 layers) and BERT Large (24 layers), visualizing the depth of the Transformer encoder stack.
teaching_value: Visually reinforces the concept of stacked Transformer encoder blocks, helping students grasp how depth differs between the two BERT variants and why depth matters for representation learning.
when_to_show: Show alongside the model architecture explanation to help students visualize the stacking of encoder blocks and compare depths between BERT variants.
suggested_caption: BERT's stacked encoder blocks: 12 (Base) vs. 24 (Large) layers. (Alammar)

description: A diagram showing BERT's input-output structure: a sequence of word tokens (with [CLS] as the first token) fed into the encoder stack, producing a corresponding sequence of output vectors.
teaching_value: Clarifies the role of the special [CLS] token and shows how BERT maps each input token to an output vector, helping students understand the input format and output structure.
when_to_show: Show when explaining BERT's input requirements and output structure, especially before discussing how the [CLS] output is used for classification.
suggested_caption: BERT input sequence with [CLS] token produces per-token output vectors. (Alammar)

description: A diagram illustrating how input tokens flow upward through BERT's stacked encoder layers, with each layer applying self-attention and feed-forward operations before passing results to the next layer.
teaching_value: Reinforces the sequential, layer-by-layer processing of tokens through the encoder stack, helping students understand how representations are refined at each layer via self-attention.
when_to_show: Show when explaining the internal mechanics of BERT's encoder stack to illustrate how information flows and transforms through the network.
suggested_caption: Tokens flow through BERT's encoder layers via self-attention and feed-forward networks. (Alammar)

description: A diagram highlighting the output vector at the [CLS] position (first token) from BERT's final encoder layer, showing it as a 768-dimensional vector used as input to a downstream classifier.
teaching_value: Concretely shows students which output vector is used for classification tasks and its dimensionality (768 for BERT Base), bridging the gap between BERT's output and the classifier head.
when_to_show: Show when explaining how BERT's output is connected to a classifier, particularly to clarify why only the [CLS] position output is used for sentence-level tasks.
suggested_caption: BERT's [CLS] output: a 768-dim vector fed to the classifier. (Alammar)

description: A diagram showing a single-layer neural network classifier receiving the [CLS] output vector from BERT and producing classification probabilities (e.g., spam/not spam) via softmax.
teaching_value: Illustrates the simplicity of the classification head on top of BERT, showing students that powerful results can be achieved with minimal additional architecture after fine-tuning.
when_to_show: Show when discussing fine-tuning and the classifier architecture to emphasize how lightweight the task-specific component is relative to the pre-trained BERT model.
suggested_caption: A simple single-layer classifier on BERT's output achieves strong results. (Alammar)

description: A diagram of VGGNet architecture showing the convolutional feature extraction layers followed by fully-connected classification layers, used as an analogy to BERT + classifier head.
teaching_value: Helps students with computer vision backgrounds map the familiar CNN feature extractor + classifier pattern onto BERT's pre-trained encoder + classifier head, making transfer learning intuitive.
when_to_show: Show when drawing the parallel between BERT and CNNs to help students with vision backgrounds understand the transfer learning paradigm by analogy.
suggested_caption: VGGNet's conv+classifier mirrors BERT's encoder+head structure. (Alammar)

description: A diagram showing GloVe/Word2Vec-style static word embeddings, where each word maps to a fixed vector regardless of context, illustrating the traditional word representation approach.
teaching_value: Establishes the baseline of static word embeddings so students can appreciate the key limitation (context-insensitivity) that contextual models like BERT overcome.
when_to_show: Show before introducing contextual embeddings (ELMo, BERT) to contrast static word vectors with context-dependent representations and motivate the need for models like BERT.
suggested_caption: GloVe assigns fixed vectors to words, ignoring context. (Alammar)

description: A visual representation of a word embedding as a colored rectangular vector box, showing how a word maps to a list of numbers (a dense vector).
teaching_value: Gives students a concrete visual shorthand for word vectors, making abstract numeric representations tangible before introducing more complex embedding models.
when_to_show: Show at the very start of any lesson on word representations or before introducing Word2Vec/GloVe to establish the visual language used throughout the article.
suggested_caption: A word represented as a dense numeric vector (embedding). — Jay Alammar

description: Diagram contrasting a static GloVe embedding for the word 'stick' with ELMo's context-sensitive embedding, illustrating that ELMo assigns different vectors to the same word depending on surrounding context.
teaching_value: Directly illustrates the core limitation of static embeddings (one vector per word regardless of meaning) versus contextualized embeddings, motivating why ELMo was a breakthrough.
when_to_show: Show when introducing the concept of polysemy and the motivation for contextualized embeddings, after students understand static embeddings.
suggested_caption: ELMo assigns different embeddings to 'stick' based on context. — Jay Alammar

description: Visualization of a GloVe word embedding for the word 'stick' as a long vector of numbers, demonstrating the fixed, context-independent nature of static word embeddings.
teaching_value: Concretizes the concept of a static embedding — the same vector is always used for 'stick' regardless of meaning — setting up the contrast with contextualized embeddings.
when_to_show: Show when explaining the limitation of fixed embeddings, just before introducing ELMo and contextualized representations.
suggested_caption: GloVe gives 'stick' one fixed vector regardless of meaning. — Jay Alammar

description: Diagram of ELMo's bi-directional LSTM architecture performing language modeling, showing hidden states emerging from each LSTM step and being used to form embeddings.
teaching_value: Illustrates how ELMo's pre-training objective (next-word prediction via language modeling) produces hidden states that encode contextual information, explaining the source of ELMo's representational power.
when_to_show: Show when explaining ELMo's pre-training mechanism and how hidden states from an LSTM become the basis for contextualized embeddings.
suggested_caption: ELMo's bi-directional LSTM hidden states power its embeddings. — Jay Alammar

description: Step-by-step diagram of ELMo's forward and backward language model pre-training, showing the input sequence 'Let's stick to' with the model predicting the next word in both directions.
teaching_value: Shows concretely how bidirectional language modeling works — the model reads text both forward and backward — helping students understand why ELMo captures richer context than unidirectional models.
when_to_show: Show when explaining the difference between unidirectional and bidirectional language models, and how ELMo's training objective differs from GPT.
suggested_caption: ELMo trains forward and backward LSTMs simultaneously for context. — Jay Alammar

description: Diagram showing how ELMo combines hidden states from multiple LSTM layers (via concatenation and weighted summation) to produce a final contextualized word embedding.
teaching_value: Explains ELMo's embedding construction process — that the final embedding is not just one layer's output but a learned combination of all layers — helping students understand why ELMo captures both syntactic and semantic information.
when_to_show: Show after explaining ELMo's LSTM architecture, when discussing how the pre-trained hidden states are converted into usable word embeddings for downstream tasks.
suggested_caption: ELMo combines all LSTM layer hidden states into one embedding. — Jay Alammar

description: Architecture diagram of the OpenAI GPT model showing a stack of 12 Transformer decoder layers (without encoder-decoder attention) used for language model pre-training.
teaching_value: Visually establishes the GPT architecture — decoder-only Transformer stack — helping students understand how the encoder-decoder structure was simplified for language modeling and transfer learning.
when_to_show: Show when introducing GPT and contrasting it with the full encoder-decoder Transformer, emphasizing the architectural choice of using only the decoder.
suggested_caption: GPT stacks 12 Transformer decoder layers for language modeling. — Jay Alammar

description: Diagram of the OpenAI Transformer performing language modeling pre-training, showing masked self-attention processing a sequence of tokens to predict the next word.
teaching_value: Illustrates how masked self-attention enables autoregressive language modeling — the model can only attend to past tokens — making the pre-training objective and its mechanism concrete for students.
when_to_show: Show when explaining how GPT's masked self-attention differs from BERT's bidirectional attention, and why masking is necessary for language modeling.
suggested_caption: GPT uses masked self-attention to predict the next word. — Jay Alammar

description: Diagram showing how the pre-trained OpenAI Transformer is fine-tuned for sentence classification by adding a classification layer on top of the final decoder output.
teaching_value: Demonstrates the transfer learning workflow — pre-train then fine-tune — showing students exactly how a pre-trained language model is adapted to a supervised downstream task like spam classification.
when_to_show: Show when introducing the concept of fine-tuning pre-trained models for downstream tasks, as a concrete example before discussing BERT's approach.
suggested_caption: Fine-tuning GPT for sentence classification adds one output layer. — Jay Alammar

description: Diagram showing multiple input transformation schemes for the OpenAI Transformer, illustrating how different NLP tasks (classification, entailment, similarity, multiple choice) are reformatted as linear token sequences with special delimiter tokens.
teaching_value: Shows students how diverse NLP tasks are unified into a single input format for GPT fine-tuning, demonstrating the practical engineering of task-agnostic pre-trained models.
when_to_show: Show when discussing how GPT handles multiple NLP tasks with a single architecture, and as a contrast to BERT's approach using [CLS] and [SEP] tokens.
suggested_caption: GPT reformats diverse NLP tasks as token sequences with delimiters. — Jay Alammar

description: Diagram showing BERT's Masked Language Model (MLM) pre-training objective, where 15% of input tokens are masked with [MASK] tokens and the model must predict the original words from bidirectional context
teaching_value: Concretely illustrates why MLM solves the bidirectional conditioning problem — students can see how masking prevents a word from 'seeing itself' while still allowing full bidirectional attention across the rest of the sequence
when_to_show: When explaining why BERT uses masking instead of standard left-to-right language modeling; use to contrast with GPT's unidirectional approach
suggested_caption: BERT's Masked LM: predict hidden tokens using bidirectional context. (Alammar)

description: Diagram illustrating BERT's Next Sentence Prediction (NSP) pre-training task, showing two input sentences (A and B) fed into BERT with a binary classification output indicating whether B follows A
teaching_value: Shows students how BERT is pre-trained to understand inter-sentence relationships, explaining why it can handle downstream tasks like question answering and natural language inference that require reasoning across sentence pairs
when_to_show: When introducing BERT's two pre-training tasks; pair with the MLM diagram to give a complete picture of BERT's pre-training strategy before discussing fine-tuning
suggested_caption: BERT's Next Sentence Prediction pre-training task for sentence-pair understanding. (Alammar)

description: Grid or panel diagram showing how BERT is adapted for multiple downstream NLP tasks (e.g., classification, NER, question answering, sentence-pair tasks) by adding task-specific output layers on top of the pre-trained model
teaching_value: Demonstrates the versatility of BERT's fine-tuning paradigm — students can see that the same pre-trained model backbone handles structurally different tasks by only changing the output head, reinforcing the concept of transfer learning
when_to_show: After explaining pre-training; use when discussing how one model generalizes across NLP benchmarks, and to contrast fine-tuning with feature extraction
suggested_caption: BERT fine-tuned for four distinct NLP task types with minimal modification. (Alammar)

description: Diagram comparing different strategies for extracting contextualized embeddings from BERT's layers (e.g., last layer, second-to-last layer, sum of last four layers, concatenation of last four layers) with performance scores on a benchmark task
teaching_value: Helps students understand that BERT's internal representations vary across layers and that choosing which layer(s) to extract embeddings from is a meaningful design decision with measurable performance impact
when_to_show: When contrasting fine-tuning vs. feature extraction use of BERT; use to prompt discussion about what different transformer layers encode
suggested_caption: Comparing BERT layer extraction strategies for contextualized embeddings on NER. (Alammar)

description: Diagram showing the feature extraction pipeline where a frozen pre-trained BERT model generates contextualized token embeddings that are then fed into a separate downstream model (e.g., for named entity recognition)
teaching_value: Illustrates the alternative to fine-tuning — students see that BERT can act as a static feature extractor like ELMo, clarifying the distinction between two major paradigms for applying pre-trained language models
when_to_show: When teaching the two modes of using pre-trained models (fine-tuning vs. feature extraction); effective for students familiar with ELMo who are learning how BERT extends that paradigm
suggested_caption: BERT as a frozen feature extractor feeding embeddings into a downstream model. (Alammar)

description: Neural network architecture diagram of the skip-gram model showing a one-hot encoded input vector x of size V, a hidden layer of size N representing the word embedding, weight matrices W (V×N) and W' (N×V), and a softmax output layer producing a one-hot encoded output vector y of size V.
teaching_value: Visually maps the data flow from a one-hot word representation through a compressed embedding layer and back to a vocabulary-sized probability distribution, making the dimensionality reduction and the role of weight matrices W and W' concrete and traceable.
when_to_show: Show when introducing the skip-gram model, specifically when explaining how a single target word is used to predict a context word and how the hidden layer encodes the word embedding of size N.
suggested_caption: Skip-gram model: one-hot input → N-dim embedding → softmax output. (Weng, 2017)

description: Neural network architecture diagram of the CBOW model showing multiple one-hot encoded context word input vectors, individual projections through weight matrix W into N-dimensional vectors, an averaging operation in the hidden layer to produce a single fixed-length vector, and a softmax output layer predicting the target word.
teaching_value: Contrasts with the skip-gram diagram by showing how multiple context words are each projected and then averaged before prediction, making the averaging step and its smoothing effect on distributional information visually explicit and directly comparable to skip-gram.
when_to_show: Show immediately after the skip-gram diagram when comparing the two word2vec architectures, emphasizing the reversed prediction direction (context → target) and the averaging of multiple input vectors in the hidden layer.
suggested_caption: CBOW model: multiple context vectors averaged → predict target word. (Weng, 2017)

description: Binary tree diagram illustrating hierarchical softmax, with gray internal nodes representing probability branch points, white leaf nodes representing vocabulary words, and a highlighted path from the root to a specific leaf word w_i, with nodes along the path labeled n(w_i, j) for the j-th node.
teaching_value: Makes the computational efficiency gain of hierarchical softmax tangible by showing that predicting a word requires only O(log V) binary decisions along a tree path rather than a full softmax over all V vocabulary words, and clarifies the role of inner nodes as probability estimators.
when_to_show: Show when explaining why standard softmax is computationally impractical for large vocabularies and introducing hierarchical softmax as a solution, using the path notation n(w_i, j) to walk through how a single word's probability is computed.
suggested_caption: Hierarchical softmax tree: O(log V) path replaces full softmax. (Weng, 2017)

description: A flowchart of the paper’s end-to-end benchmark pipeline: UltraDomain documents from six domains are segmented by 36 chunking strategies (six design categories), embedded by five dense embedding models, indexed into separate Qdrant vector stores (1,080 total configurations), then evaluated per query by retrieving top-5 chunks and scoring them with a Mixtral-8x22B LLM judge against a gold reference using a 3-point graded relevance scale; the query text is hidden from the judge to reduce lexical bias; efficiency metrics (index size, latency, memory) are recorded in parallel; no generation step is included.
teaching_value: Helps students understand how to design a controlled retrieval benchmark: isolating the chunking variable, enumerating configurations, separating indexing from evaluation, using graded relevance for ranking metrics, and measuring effectiveness and efficiency simultaneously while avoiding evaluation leakage/bias.
concepts: evaluation leakage and lexical bias control
when_to_show: Show when a learner asks how to set up an experiment comparing chunking methods, or when explaining why retrieval-only evaluation (no generation) is needed to attribute effects to segmentation and embeddings.
suggested_caption: End-to-end chunking benchmark pipeline (arXiv, Fig. 1).

description: A boxplot distribution chart of nDCG@5 across all chunking strategies aggregated over domains and embedding models, ordered by descending mean nDCG@5. Red diamonds mark means and black lines mark medians; box/whisker spread indicates variability across queries. Top-performing methods (e.g., PGC, HPGC, DFC) appear on the left with higher central tendency, while weaker methods (e.g., FC, FCC, HFCF) appear on the right with lower scores and differing stability.
teaching_value: Teaches how to interpret retrieval metric distributions beyond averages: comparing central tendency (mean/median) and robustness (variance/whiskers) to identify strategies that are both effective and stable across queries.
when_to_show: Show when discussing why mean nDCG alone can be misleading, or when selecting a chunking strategy that avoids catastrophic failures on hard queries.
suggested_caption: nDCG@5 distributions by chunking strategy (arXiv, Fig. 2).

description: A heatmap of mean nDCG@5 by chunking strategy (x-axis) and domain (y-axis: Agriculture, Biology, Health, Legal, Maths, Physics). It highlights domain-specific peaks (e.g., DFC highest in Health and Physics) and consistently strong methods across domains (PGC, HPGC, LCTS frequently in the top tier).
teaching_value: Helps students see that chunking is domain-dependent: the same segmentation choice can perform differently across document types, motivating per-domain validation and explaining why structure-aware/late chunking can generalize better.
when_to_show: Show when a learner asks whether one chunking strategy works for all corpora, or when planning evaluation across multiple knowledge domains.
suggested_caption: Domain-wise mean nDCG@5 by chunking method (arXiv, Fig. 3).

description: A heatmap of mean nDCG@5 across five embedding models (rows) and 36 chunking strategies (columns). It shows bge-m3 as the strongest overall model (highest average), all-MiniLM-L6-v2 as second, and potion-base variants trailing; performance varies by chunking method, indicating interactions between model capacity and segmentation.
teaching_value: Illustrates that embedding model choice affects absolute performance but does not eliminate the need for good chunking; students learn to look for model–chunking interactions and to avoid attributing gains solely to model size.
when_to_show: Show when choosing an embedding model for a retrieval system, or when explaining why benchmarking should vary both chunking and embeddings to avoid confounding.
suggested_caption: Mean nDCG@5 across embedding models and chunking (arXiv, Fig. 4).

description: Two scatter plots showing effectiveness–efficiency trade-offs by chunking strategy: (left) mean nDCG@5 versus index size (index growth/cost), and (right) mean nDCG@5 versus query latency (response time). Points represent chunking methods, revealing which strategies achieve high effectiveness with smaller indexes and/or lower latency, and which incur high cost for limited gains.
teaching_value: Teaches practical system design: selecting chunking strategies requires balancing retrieval quality against operational constraints (storage and latency), and visualizing trade-offs helps identify dominated methods and near-Pareto-optimal choices.
when_to_show: Show when a learner needs to pick a chunking strategy under memory/latency budgets, or when introducing Pareto-front thinking for retrieval system optimization.
suggested_caption: Trade-offs: nDCG@5 vs index size and latency (arXiv, Fig. 5).

description: A Pearson correlation matrix (heatmap) across evaluation metrics, showing strong correlations among ranking metrics (nDCG@5 highly correlated with MRR@5 and Precision@5), weak correlation between embedding dimensionality and performance (|r|<0.2), and expected negative relationships among efficiency metrics (index size, latency) and effectiveness in some regions.
teaching_value: Helps students validate metric choices and avoid redundant reporting: it demonstrates that nDCG@5 aligns with other ranking metrics, while efficiency metrics capture different information, supporting multi-objective evaluation.
when_to_show: Show when justifying why nDCG@5 is the primary metric, or when teaching how to interpret correlations to select a compact but informative metric set.
suggested_caption: Correlation structure of effectiveness and efficiency metrics (arXiv, Fig. 6).
